title: IBM chip-design 'breakthrough' weakens Huawei claims
gist: IBM's new 'nanostack' 3D-transistor architecture — claiming sub-nanometer (0.7nm/7-angstrom) chips — undercuts Huawei's month-earlier 'Tau Scaling Law' (Her's Law) claim of 3D chip stacking, since Huawei still cannot access EUV lithography and remains stuck around 7nm using DUV plus multiple patterning, while IBM faces no such sanctions constraint.

themes.theme: Huawei's 'Her's Law' claim vs. IBM's nanostack

insights.claim: Huawei announced a 3D chip-stacking breakthrough it dubbed the 'Tau Scaling Law' or 'Her's Law' (after the responsible Huawei executive), claiming it could let Huawei match, though not replicate, the performance of 3nm chips despite being stuck at roughly 7nm.
insights.speaker: Light Reading (via SemiWiki)
insights.so_what: Huawei's claim was explicitly a workaround for sanctions-driven node limitations rather than a genuine node-equivalent breakthrough — investors should discount Huawei's competitive claims against TSMC/Samsung leading-edge output accordingly.
insights.quote: Her's Law wouldn't necessarily allow Huawei to produce the 3-nanometer chips used in iPhones and entering network products made by Ericsson, according to a company representative. But it might allow Huawei to achieve an equivalent performance and remain competitive.

insights.claim: IBM announced its own 3D 'nanostack' architecture, vertically stacking and staggering transistors via 3D sequential integration, and is marketing it as the world's first sub-nanometer chip at roughly 0.7 nanometers (7 angstroms).
insights.speaker: Light Reading (via SemiWiki)
insights.so_what: IBM's claimed 7-angstrom dimension, if it approaches commercial viability, would leapfrog the entire industry's current 2nm roadmap — a marker for IBM's re-entry into cutting-edge process relevance versus TSMC/Intel/Samsung.
insights.quote: The nanostack design vertically stacks and staggers transistors, taking advantage of 3D sequential integration to pack more transistors onto a chip.

insights.claim: Unlike Huawei, IBM is not the target of sanctions and can access EUV lithography from ASML, making its 3D-stacking claims more credible as an actual density/performance path rather than a sanctions workaround.
insights.speaker: Light Reading (via SemiWiki)
insights.so_what: The credibility gap between IBM's and Huawei's '3D breakthrough' claims stems directly from export-control access to EUV — a reminder that Huawei's technical claims should be read through the lens of sanctions-driven necessity, not parity.
insights.quote: But IBM has not, of course, been the target of crippling sanctions.

themes.theme: Node reality: Huawei stuck at 7nm via DUV multi-patterning

insights.claim: TSMC currently ships 3-nanometer chips at high volume (customers include Ericsson), while Huawei and its Chinese partners remain stuck at roughly 7 nanometers, about two process generations behind, due to being cut off from advanced chipmaking equipment.
insights.speaker: Light Reading (via SemiWiki)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the node gap (2 generations, 3nm vs 7nm) between TSMC and Huawei's Chinese supply chain — a concrete anchor for assessing how far US export controls have set back Chinese leading-edge chip capability.
insights.quote: Huawei and its Chinese partners appear stuck on 7 nanometers, about two generations behind.

insights.claim: Because ASML is barred from selling EUV to China, Huawei's Chinese supply chain (via SMIC) reportedly achieves 7nm using older deep ultraviolet (DUV) lithography combined with multiple patterning, a technique that typically lowers yield.
insights.speaker: Light Reading (via SemiWiki)
insights.so_what: Confirms the yield penalty mechanism behind China's sanctioned-node production — relevant for modeling Huawei/SMIC chip cost structure and output volumes versus EUV-equipped competitors.
insights.quote: Industry sources reckon the Chinese company relied on deep ultra-violet lithography (DUV), an older generation, in tandem with a technique called multiple patterning, which effectively repeats the lithography process.
insights.locator: paragraph on going atomic, EUV discussion

insights.claim: Huawei has stated it expects to be able to produce chips with 1.4-nanometer-equivalent capability by 2031, a claim that met with skepticism when Her's Law was unveiled in late May.
insights.speaker: Light Reading (via SemiWiki)
insights.so_what: A specific, falsifiable long-range target (1.4nm-equivalent by 2031) to benchmark Huawei's future claims against, especially given industry skepticism already expressed at unveiling.
insights.quote: By 2031, the vendor reckons it will be able to produce chips with the capability of 1.4-nanometer designs.
